title: How NOT To Inject The Orbit: Vital Tear Trough Anatomy Tutorial [Aesthetics Mastery Show]
filename: How NOT To Inject The Orbit： Vital Tear Trough Anatomy Tutorial [Aesthetics Mastery Show].en.vtt
excerpt: We hope you found that do drop us a note below but all the anatomy that you might have good we welcome to the ascetics mastery show I'm doctor Tim Pierce hi I'm Randy Pierce and today we are talking about the weird complication doctor master told us about on a live webinar recent…

segments.text: we hope you found that do drop us a note below but all the anatomy that you might have good we welcome to the ascetics mastery show I'm doctor Tim Pierce hi I'm Randy Pierce and today we are talking about the weird complication doctor master told us about on a live webinar recently we'll put that link below so you can watch that we're talking essentially about when filler gets into the orbit instead of into the pre-psychotic space a few weeks ago we discussed edema and other complications from tear troughs how is this different so the difference with this complication is you've actually got dermal filler behind the orbital septum and the orbital septum is a natural barriers actually there too probably to keep infection separate so if you've got an infection on the surface of your skin it wouldn't go into orbit quite as easily so now it's a natural barrier

which means if you get dermal filler behind it you may actually hold on to that in theory for many years and that's certainly been dr. masters experience and it's something I think is probably happening out there and people are miss diagnosing it which is why I think it's a really important show for people to watch so you've done a plus C models shows anatomy yeah so earlier today I built out this another focus because you can build these skulls in many different ways the focus of this one is to show you the different compartments so we're going to do a close-up of that I'll talk you through some of the anatomy and then show you where the needle is passing and how easy it is to get into the wrong place basically before we go there just let me ask about that skip to the end and just say if you do get some filler into that it's the orbit the reason that you were talking about this with dr. master is that then it's incredibly hard to reverse well it may not be hard to reverse but it's risky to reverse because you're essentially saying I'm going through your orbital septum and that immediately means you're

much closer to the globe and it does make sense that if you have certainly if you've got easy access to an ultrasound machine do it under Alta Sun guidance now there are people who have experienced with the orb who will be happy to do without that because I know that's not necessarily how they would do any procedure near that area but yeah let us know big interesting to know people's experience especially if you're an oculoplastic surgeon would you do it under ultrasound guidance or would you happily do it knowing your Anatomy because I think some people would okay let's talk prevention first of all okay well prevention is ultimately all about your anatomy and I'd like we said in many of the previous episodes and that's me as your is number one in terms of designing your injection technique number two is the aesthetic result but we've got to be safe first and so what you're trying to do with your injection technique is choose a technique that is likely to minimize the risk of ending up in the wrong place okay that starts with understanding the anatomy so let's have it let's have a look at all these structures here structures to know are

the zygomatic retaining ligament here which runs on obviously on the anterior surface of the zygoma and then you have the orbicularis oculi retaining ligament which runs around from around the orbit so if you have a look behind here and have a look straight into that area you can see we're actually within the orbit here and I'm resting this instrument on the globe this is very firm on me and the orbital rim I can feel that very firmly and this is the key to understand we've got all the cool rim coming off it the orbicularis oculi retaining ligament underneath that is the sooth and that's usually the area that we are replacing bogeymen so we're injecting filler to replace volume in the suit as you get older that tends to get smaller and some people have protrudes out that's one of the things to understand about the tear trough is you're really trying to harmonize these three sets of fat pads which is the medial cheek fat pad the sooth and then the intraocular fat pad can also create a shadow and some people obviously we're not going to do much about that it might

be surgery that's best to remove some of that fat and but you might be able to harmonize it if they've got lost volume in this fat pad here so okay so this little structure here is the orbital septum and the orbital septum bridges over these two areas so it connects with the lower eyelid and it comes off the orbicularis oculi retaining ligament released from the same place and beneath that is the intra orbital fat but this is where we don't want them or for that this is where these complications arises that somehow we're getting further into this space now if you actually think about how this how easy this will be if you imagine using a cannula and you're inserting it here just superior to the zygomatic ligaments you might be superior to it or inferior to it doesn't really matter and because at some point you've got to have to go into the soup which is here so if you're trying to inject the sooth you can see how easy it would be to just go a little bit too superior and pop into there so if you can just have a look you can see the tip of that needle

is actually in that space so we're now we are within the orbit if you add a dandy filler here you're injecting straight into the orbit and even though it might be a matter of millimeters that's very similar to how you might be injecting now maybe you were going a little bit higher up but the still room is still possible to get up wherever you are if you're injecting in and out with that cannula making multiple passes it could very easily slip into that into the orbit and then you add a bit of product which then causes problems years later and we give just give you a bit more and ask me to understand the other side so we have the superficial the infraorbital fat pad which sits on top of a bicular oculi underneath that you have the orbital septum superiorly and underneath that you'll have the sooth so inferior to that you have the tzuf superior to a ligament you have the orbit itself which is covered with the orbital septum so let's talk about how dermal filler injections are often done in the tear trough and I'll just show you roughly speaking this is obviously not my normal

angle but where you have the zygomatic retaining ligament here and the orbicularis oculi rotating ligament here this is where you get your tear trough in this tiny space in between the two and you're effectively replacing the sooth if you're using a cannula you're angled up like this and you're seeing the tip of the cannula move but you're also trying to be deep and if you're trying to be deep it's very easy with one of these sweeps if you have a thin membrane to pop through it and as soon as you pop through it as you're going to see on the next model you're in the orbit and then if you add product here you're going to have a problem so the other way people treat is with a needle and this is to the periosteum now the advantage with this is if you're on the periosteum you shouldn't be in the orbit so it's a pretty certain way to know where you are the downside is you can be very close to it and pop in make a little hole quite easily so you need to be practicing your angles and so that it's very hard for you to be angled slightly differently and get into the wrong place you can see how that angle might make a difference in terms of whether you're in the orbit or whether you're on the periosteum so

slow injecting just kissing the periosteum before you inject being nice and stable small amounts at a time and by the way I'm not going into teaching you how to do teardrop on this is just about the concept of where you're injecting and how easy it is to pop into the orbit I'm also aware of one case of blindness that was caused by injecting laterally where it slid into the orbit as well an injected certainly affected the lateral rectus and there was temporary loss of vision as well so this is all down to injecting with not particularly well thought-out angles with respect to protecting the orbit so what can we do to be safer injectors you just want to be feeling this area before you inject you want to make sure you know where the orbital room is it isn't always intuitive sometimes you think you're going to find the orbit in one place and it's half as its it's kind of five millimeters lower or higher than you suspect so it's a really good idea to feel it and then while you're injecting if you're using a cannula you

could actually place your finger and over that area to protect the orbit so that you're unable to go to Superior and into that went too deep into the orbit thank you for watching let us know what you think in the comments down below and if you'd like to download a picture of the anatomy of the orbit so that you can think how these different compartments are divided up and improve your injection technique click on the link down below take care bye [Music]
